text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Whipple procedure, beginning with the excision of the falciform ligament to access the lesser sac. The inferior and superior surfaces of the pancreas are identified, and orientation around the gastrocolic trunk is established. The Thunderbeat device is used to manage branches of the gastrocolic trunk, which is clipped and divided. Bleeding occurs due to clip placement but is controlled with the Thunderbeat. The tunnel of the portal vein posterior to the pancreatic neck is dissected, providing a clear view of the portal vein. The pars flaccida is opened posterior to the stomach, distal to the pylorus, revealing the duodenum, and the right gastric vessels are incorporated.

The surgeon inserts a Nathanson liver retractor and begins dissection superior to the pancreas. The hepatic artery is visible, dissected from the superior edge of the pancreas. The gastroduodenal artery is isolated, and its occlusion confirms a good pulse up into the liver. A clean staple line is visible with the pancreas located posteriorly.

The surgeon ties and clips the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) to prevent a pseudoaneurysm, using the ThunderBeat for triple ligation. The pancreatic neck tunnel is visualized, with a grasper positioned across it. The Coker maneuver is performed, starting inferior to the mesentery, further mobilizing the duodenum. The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visible, indicating the safest dissection area is directly on the IVC.

The surgeon inserts the camera through the right flank, providing clear visualization of the duodenum posteriorly. The left renal vein is visible as it inserts into the inferior vena cava (IVC), with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) takeoff appearing above. The pulse is observable through the vein. The surgeon opens the ligament of Treitz and uses cautery to take the attachments, creating a window to the left side of the mesentery. A suitable place to divide the small bowel is identified, and the surgeon staples across the bowel from the patient's right side of the mesentery.

The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery of the small bowel to isolate the uncinate process. The superior mesenteric vein is visible extending to the pancreatic neck tunnel. Branches of the mesentery and uncinate process are dissected. The pancreatic neck is transected using the Thunderbeat, achieving excellent hemostasis. The uncinate process is further mobilized, with visible oozy bleeding from the SMV and portal vein side branches. The SMA is visible, pulsing, with small branches off the vein and artery, which are clipped or transected using the Thunderbeat.

The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat to control nuisance bleeding and continues dissection around the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), which is visibly pulsing. Residual attachments are removed primarily with the Thunder Beat, and clips are applied as needed. The vascular groove is peeled away, leaving lymphatic attachments to the right branch of the hepatic artery. The bile duct is isolated, and the gallbladder is mobilized from its confluence with the common hepatic duct. The cystic duct is retracted laterally, and the common bile duct is sharply divided distal to the cystic duct insertion. The duct measures approximately five millimeters in diameter, indicating a good blood supply and suitability for anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a lateral view to position the bowel through the retro mesenteric window, ensuring it is oriented without twists. The pancreas is examined for the pancreatic duct, but it is not visible. The surgeon uses scissors to cut the transected end of the pancreas in an attempt to locate the duct, but is unsuccessful. The pancreas is further mobilized in preparation for a pancreaticogastrostomy. The bowel is opened with the Thunder Beat, and a corner stay suture is placed with IV-O polysorb, which is then used to run a closure of the back wall.

The surgeon completes the back wall of the anastomosis, ensuring the duct remains open. A running suture is used to close the bile duct side, creating a secure hepatico-jejunostomy. Clean white gauze sponges are placed to check for biliary leaks, and upon removal, no leaks are observed. A Jackson-Pratt drain is positioned, and the Nathanson retractor is released.
